text:
Admission Date:  1974-07-14              Discharge Date:   1991-04-01

Date of Birth:  1978-01-14             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Christopher
Chief Complaint:
Fevers.

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None.

History of Present Illness:
Mr. Young is a 50 y.o. man DM and HTN with a left foot diabetic
ulcer who presented to the ED after being discharged the same
day from Viera Hospital and developing fevers up to 103 on the way to
rehab.

His recent history is significant for an admission from 12-27 to
09-19 for left foot osteomyelitis for which he was treated with
vancomycin and fluconazole.  He was re-admitted from 11-01 to 02-03
with fevers thought to be due to a PICC line infection.  His
PICC was replaced and he was re-started on vancomycin, continued
on his fluconazole, and started on a two week course of
levofloxacin.  On his way to rehab on 11-21 he developed fevers to
103 and chills and re-presented to the Viera Hospital ED.  He endorsed
fatigue but no other complaints.

In the ED he was febrile to 102.8 and started on Vanco, Levo,
and Flagyl. Overnight, his SBP dropped into the 80s and after
receiving 5-6 L NS he was started on peripheral dopamine gtt
with response.  He was initially admitted to the MICU and
started on broad spectrum antibiotics (caspofungin for [**Female First Name (un) **],
ceftazidime for h/o pseudomonas infection, vanco for MSRA).  He
was also started on flagyl for complaints of increasing diarrhea
and leukocytosis (c.diff positive on 11-21).  He required pressor
support for some time and was also started on
hydrocort/fludrocort for a low random cortisol (9.2).


Past Medical History:
1) HTN
2) Type II DM
3) GERD
4) Diabetic foot ulcer: s/p first MP joint fusion. s/p
rotational flap closure
5) [**Month/Day (2) 8304**] LBP
6) HCV
7) h/o EtOH abuse
8) s/p splenectomy


Social History:
Retired, lives with brother. Denies tobacco or other drug use.
No current EtOH (prior heavy use)


Family History:
Non-contributory

Physical Exam:
PE Temp (rectal 102)  P 118  BP 115/69  R 18  O2 99% on RA
Gen - A+Ox3(mildly confused)  NAD
HEENT - EOMI, OP clear, non icteric scleara, scab on top of L
side of head.  no erythema, no drainage
Cor - RRR no m/r/g
Lungs - CTA B although did not cooperate fully with exam
Back - no CVA tend, no spinal tend
Ext - no edema, L foot with dorsal loose suture line, c/d/i,
plantar puncture wound oozing
Neuro - CN II - XII, nl strength/Estrada x 4 ext
Skin - no rash


Pertinent Results:
Pertinent Labs:
WBC Trend:
 1991-04-01  11.1
 1988-03-23  17.0
 1989-05-28  28.2
 2007-08-21  26.8
 1974-07-14  29.1
 1974-07-14  34.7
 1989-09-29  21.5

Creatinine: 2.2 initially -> 0.9 for final four days of hospital
stay
LFTs on 1988-03-23 ALT 68 AST 44 LDH 125 Alk Phos 128 T. bili 0.3

Microbiology:
02-03, 9/2 Blood cultures:  Pending at discharge.
11-21 Stool culture: FECES POSITIVE FOR C. DIFFICILE TOXIN BY EIA.
Studies:
02-03 CXR:
The heart, mediastinal, and hilar contours are within normal
limits. The lungs are clear. The osseous structures are stable.
11-21 LEFT FOOT X-RAY:
Postoperative changes at the first MTP remains similar in
appearance since the prior examination, with scattered osseous
fragments and soft tissue swelling. No large collection of air
is detected within the soft tissues. The defects of the fifth
MTP is also unchanged. Prominent atherosclerotic calcifications
are noted throughout the visualized soft tissues. A large
plantar spur is unchanged. No new fracture, dislocation, or bone
destruction.

Brief Hospital Course:
50 y.o. man with DM, HTN, h/o [**Month/Day (2) **] left foot ulcer who
presented to the ED with fevers and hypotension.

1. Sepsis/ID:
He was initially admitted to the MICU due to hypotension and
concern for septic shock.  He was initially started on broad
spectrum antibiotics (ceftazidime, caspofungin, and vancomycin).
 A stool sample was positive for c. difficile and he was started
on metronidazole as well.  His leukocytosis on admission (34.7)
also seemed consistent with c. difficile colitis.  Initially he
needed blood pressure support with pressor agents and was
started on hydrocortisone/fludrocortisone due to a low random
cortisol. After his blood pressure stablized off pressor agents
he was transferred to the regular floor on 03-02 and he remained
hemodynamically stable and afebrile until discharge on 09-02.  He
was weaned off both hydrocortisone and fludricortisone and his
blood pressure remained stable.  He was discharged with
instructions and prescriptions to complete a 14 day course of
flagyl 500 tid and a 30 day course of fluconazole 400 qd.  He
has a follow up appointment in the Mclaren Lapeer Region clinic with Dr. Hill on
10-17.

2. Left foot ulcer:
Podiatry was contactRicardo Lane about the management of his Ricardo Lane foot
ulcer and thought there were no acute issues to address during
his hospitalization.  He had daily dressing changes and did an
excellent job of staying off his feet and elevating his foot
whenever possible.  He was given a prescription to complete a 30
day course of fluconazole 400 qd and has a follow up appointment
with his podiatrist, Dr. Sanchez on 04-03.

3. Diabetes Mellitus:
This was initially controlled with an insulin drip in the MICU.
When he was transferred to the floor he was started on his home
dose of metformin 500 Va Medical Center - John Cochran Division and was put on a sliding scale. His
lisinopril 5 mg qd was also re-started.  His blood sugar control
was adequate on this regimen and he was encouraged to follow up
on this issue with his PCP.

4. Acute Renal Failure:
His creatinine was elevated initially in the ED (2.2) but
returned to his baseline (0.9) with hydration and this was
thought to be due mostly to a pre-renal state.  He was
encouraged to maintain an adequate po intake.

5. Seizures:
He had no episodes of seizure activity during this hospital
stay.  He was maintained on his keppra and encouraged to follow
up with neurology at some point in the future.

7. Disposition:
He was initially planning to go to a rehab facility but felt
increasingly stronger throughout his hospital stay and elected
to go home to stay with his brother.  Ricardo Lane also was anxious to get
home as he was unable to find a bag with multiple personal items
that he had with him when he came to the ED.  Social work was
contactRicardo Lane about this issue but his bag was not found by the time
of discharge.  He was given several phone numbers to call to
follow up on finding his bag.
At the time of discharge, he had follow up appointments arranged
with infectious disease, podiatry, and his PCP.


Discharge Medications:
1. Pantoprazole Sodium 40 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig:
One (1) Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO Q24H (every 24 hours).

2. Aspirin 81 mg Tablet, Chewable Sig: One (1) Tablet, Chewable
PO DAILY (Daily).
3. Lisinopril 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

4. Metformin 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a
day).
5. Levetiracetam 250 mg Tablet Sig: Three (3) Tablet PO BID (2
times a day).
6. Fluconazole 200 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q24H (every
24 hours) for 11 days.
Disp:*22 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
7. Metronidazole 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO TID (3
times a day) for 7 days.
Disp:*21 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
8. Oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO every six (6) hours
as needed.
Disp:*40 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary Diagnosis:
1. C. diff colitis.
2. Left foot candidal osteomyelitis.
Secondary Diagnosis:
1. Diabetes
2. Hypertension
3. Hepatitis C
4. Seizure d/o


Discharge Condition:
Stable.


Discharge Instructions:
1. Please take your medications as prescribed.
2. Please come to your followup appointments (see below).
3. Please try to stay off your left foot and keep it elevated
when you can.
4. If you have any fevers, increased swelling or redness of your

left foot, abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, or other
concerning symptoms please seek medical attention


Followup Instructions:
1. Please follow-up with your primary care provider, Young.
Mccarty Henry Pham in 10-17 weeks. His number is 833-275-9685.
You have an appointment on Friday 03-11 at 1 pm.  Feel free to
call if you need to reschedule.
